posts.title: Hello World
posts.text: Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub. Quick StartCreate a new post1$ hexo new "My New Post" More info: Writing Run server1$ hexo server More info: Server Generate static files1$ hexo generate More info: Generating Deploy to remote sites1$ hexo deploy More info: Deployment

posts.title: 3. Function approximation by global functions
posts.text: 1. Finite Element Method, FEM Form of ApproximationThe general strategy for approximating the solution u(x) to a differential equation is to express it as a sum of basis functions Principle for Constructing EquationsThe next step in solving such an equation is constructing a system of N+1 equations to solve for the coefficients C0, C1,… CN The Solve PhaseOnce the equations are constructed, the final step is solving the system for the unknown coefficients. Introduction to the Finite Element Method (FEM)In the context of FEM, a lot of mathematical machinery comes into play when constructing these equations. (ex. integration by parts, boundary conditions) A Simplified Approach Where is a given function. 3.1 Aproximation of vectors The least squares methodFinding Ci such that the difference , measured in a certain norm, is minimized. Projection = Galerkin’s methodFinding u such that the error is orthogonal to the space where u lies. 3.1.1 Approximation of planar vectors The least squares methodLet f = (3, 5) be It’s worth mentioning that setting the key equation to zero and ordering the termsor .This implication of minimizing E is an important result that we shall make much use of. posts.title: 3. From Statistical Mechanics to Thermodynamics
posts.text: 3.1 Liouville’s the theorem and equationThe total number of ensemble members is constant. So phase space volume must be equal to the rate at which the ensemble members leave through the surface S. This is exactly same conditon that we impose to derive the mass continuity equation. ex) Phase Space Equation ex) Fluid Dynamics Equation In statistical mechanics, we consider the phase space, which is a 6𝑁 dimensional space for a system with 𝑁 particles. Each point in this space is represented by: where: are the position coordinates. are the momentum coordinates. We can imagine as “velocity” in the phase space. be the ensemble density in phase spaceSo the continuity equation in phase space is .. where: is the divergence operator in the -dimensional phase space. represents the probability current in phase space. 1. Liouville equationHamiltonian dynamics can be described as an incompressible flow in phase space. 2. Liouville Operator and Poisson Brackets Liouville equation II is the Liouville operator, which can be defined using Possion brackets. Poisson bracket Thus, Liouville Equation is If the phase space distribution function (\rho(Z, t)) is conserved, it implies: Phase Space Volume Preservation 3.2 Statistical Ensembles in Equilibrium Equilibrium ConditionFor a system to be in equilibrium, the phase space distribution function should not explicitly depend on time: $\ Ergodic HypothesisThe ergodic hypothesis states that, given an infinite amount of time, an ergodic system will eventually visit all possible states that are accessible to it. For Hamiltonian systems, this implies that the system will cover all points on a hypersurface where the Hamiltonian ( H ) is constant. Ensemble and Temporal AverageIn an ergodic system, the ensemble average of a quantity is equivalent to its temporal average types of statistical ensembles The types of statistical ensembles are classified based on the quantities held constant:ex) Microcanonical Ensemble (): Represents an isolated system with fixed particle number (N), volume (V), and energy (E). The system follows Hamiltonian dynamics, and the total energy is conserved. Canonical Ensemble (): Represents a system in contact with a heat bath at a fixed temperature . The total energy of the system is not conserved because it can exchange energy with the heat bath. 3.3 Basic ThermodynamicsDefinitionsA thermodynamics system is a macroscopic system. The thermodynamic state of the system is uniquely defined by specification of a set of control variables, e.g, temperature, pressure, volume etc. Two types of thermodynamic variables: extensive variables are proportional to the system size (ex. internal energy, mass, entropy) intensive variables are independent of the system size (ex. pressure, temperature, density, chemial potential) 3.4 Laws of Thermodynamics Zeroth Law (0법칙)Establishes the concept of temperature and thermal equilibrium. If two systems are in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are also in equilibrium with each other. First Law (1법칙)The conservation energy. The energy E of the system can be changed by performing work W on it and/or by adding heat Q to it Second Law (2법칙) Third LawThe entropy of a system at the absolute zero temperature is a universal constant, which we can set to zero. The T = 0K temperature can never be physically reached.

posts.title: 2. Mathematical preliminaries
posts.text: 2.1 ConventionsT : arbitrary-order tensor. 2.2 Definition of a tensorWe can construct tensors of arbitary order n by concatenating n bases using the tensor product . For instance, a second-order tensor results from: 2.3. Tensor calculusLet’s go over the cross product definition, the role of the Levi-Civita symbol, and how they are used together to express the cross product of two vectors. 2.3.1. Cross Product DefinitionThe cross product Is perpendicular (two lines, planes, or vectors that meet at a right angle (90 degrees)) to both and The cross product in terms of vector components is given by: Levi-Civita Symbol The Levi-Civita symbol is used to simplify expressions involving cross products in three dimensions. $$[\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v}]i = \epsilon{ijk} u_j v_k$$ 2.3.5 Tensor analysis1. Gradient of a Tensor FieldThe gradient of a function is the collection of its partial derivatives. When we apply the gradient operator to a tensor field, the order of the resulting tensor increases by one. $$ [ \text{grad}, u ]{ij} = [ \nabla u ]{ij} = \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = u_{i,j}.$$ 1.1 Partial Derivative $$\frac{\partial (\cdot)}{\partial xi} = (\cdot){,i}.$$ 2. Divergence Operator The divergence of a vector or tensor field is defined. The divergence measures the “spread” or “flux” of a vector field. Divergence of a Vector Field $$\text{div}, u = \nabla \cdot u = \frac{\partial ui}{\partial x_i} = u{i,i}.$$

posts.title: 4. Entropic self-assembly
posts.text: 1. EntropyThe entropy S denotes the numbr of microstates of a system:Examples:N-fold coin toss EXAMPLEA pre-progremmed nanoparticle release cascadeAim #1: The nanoparticle (NP, for example, a drug carrier) should only be released, if the carrier matrix gets in contact with the human body.Aim #2: NP-species should be released sequentially, and from bottom to top mechanism #1: when incubated with DNA strands, silver nanoparticles (cationic) form microggregates which cannot escape from the gel matrix. However, these stable aggregates can be disintegrated by the addition of NaCl. Then, not only the silver NPs but also the DNA strands are mobilized.
